summary: Charity Majors provides a comprehensive guide for job candidates to evaluate whether a company is healthy or toxic during the interview process. She argues that candidates hold more power than they realize and should rigorously scrutinize potential employers through backchannel research, probing questions about diversity, business health, team dynamics, and engineering practices. The post emphasizes that interviews are a two-way street and encourages people to leave quickly if a new job feels wrong.
key_points: Use backchannel networks and recently-departed employees to get unvarnished truth about a company
Evaluate diversity not just in headcount but in leadership, accountability, and concrete improvement plans
Companies comfortable with visible failure and genuine transparency are self-correcting and healthier
Deploy interval is a strong proxy for engineering happiness and productivity
The questions a company asks you in interviews reveal what they actually filter for, not their advertised values
money_quotes: Your labor is valuable — it is vital — and you should be scrutinizing them every bit as closely as they you.
Being self-aware and comfortable with visible failure are two of the most important self-correcting mechanisms a company can cultivate.

EVERYBODY thinks they value transparency, so I wouldn't even bother asking. Instead, ask for specific examples of leadership being forthcoming with bad news to the team, and team members delivering hard feedback or bad news to upper leadership.

The questions they ask you while interviewing you are the questions they ask everyone else. So…did they ask you about your views on diversity and team dynamics while interviewing you? Or is that not part of their filters, only their advertised persona?
You do not owe them a year of your life. Trust your instincts.
key_insight: Job candidates should flip the interview power dynamic by rigorously evaluating companies through backchannels, probing questions about failure and diversity, and concrete signals like deploy frequency — and should leave without guilt if what they find doesn't match.
